title.emphasis: and the Enforceability of Agreements for Sexual Services

section.para: The assertion that any contract for sexual services is inherently illegal and criminal represents an oversimplification of a complex area of contract law. While agreements that are explicitly for the commission of a crime are indeed void, the California Supreme Court's landmark decision in Marvin v. Marvin established a sophisticated legal framework for analyzing agreements between unmarried partners. This framework, which hinges on the doctrine of severable consideration, provides the necessary tools to deconstruct the relationship between contracts and sexual services, creating a pathway to identify categories of agreements that may be licit and enforceable. By applying these principles, it becomes clear that the law distinguishes between a contract for prohibited sexual acts and a contract between individuals who also engage in sexual acts, a distinction that is central to this entire analysis.

title.emphasis: Doctrine: Severing Meretricious Consideration from Lawful Agreements

section.para: The foundational case for understanding the enforceability of agreements between nonmarital partners is Marvin v. Marvin (1976) 18 Cal. 3d 660. Prior to this decision, courts often refused to enforce any agreement between cohabitating partners, viewing such arrangements as "tainted" by the illicit nature of the relationship itself.22 The

Marvin court decisively rejected this approach, establishing a new, more precise standard. The court held that adults who voluntarily live together are as competent as any other individuals to contract with respect to their earnings and property rights.23

The core of the Marvin doctrine is that a contract between nonmarital partners is unenforceable only to the extent that it explicitly rests upon the immoral and illicit consideration of meretricious sexual services.23 This principle introduces the critical concept of severability. A court must examine the consideration underlying the agreement. If the agreement is not based on a promise of sexual services in exchange for financial support or property sharing, it can be enforced.26 For example, if an agreement is based on consideration such as one partner's services as a homemaker, companion, and business assistant, it is not invalidated merely because the parties also had a sexual relationship.26 The sexual relationship is seen as independent of the contractual obligations.

This legal reasoning creates a fundamental dichotomy: a contract for sex versus a contract between people who also have sex. The former, where sexual services form the basis of the bargain, is void as against public policy. The latter, where the consideration is severable and lawful in its own right, is potentially valid and enforceable, either as an express or an implied contract.23 The court in

Marvin mandated a granular, fact-based inquiry into the nature of the agreement, moving legal analysis away from a blanket prohibition based on the parties' relationship status. This forces a distinction between the relationship itself and any economic agreements made within it. This judicial approach provides the central pillar supporting the inquiry into potentially licit sexual service contracts. It requires a rigorous analysis of what, precisely, is being exchanged as consideration, rather than allowing the mere presence of a sexual component to invalidate an entire economic arrangement.

title.emphasis: to Modern Cohabitation Agreements: A Framework for Permissible Contracts

section.para: The principles articulated in Marvin have been operationalized and normalized in modern legal practice through the use of cohabitation agreements. These are legally binding contracts entered into by unmarried couples who live together, designed to define their respective property rights and financial responsibilities both during the relationship and in the event of a separation.29 These agreements are a direct and practical application of the
Marvin doctrine, demonstrating that the law has a robust mechanism for recognizing and enforcing complex financial arrangements that exist alongside a nonmarital sexual relationship.

For a cohabitation agreement to be valid and enforceable in California, it must meet standard contractual requirements. It should be in writing, involve a full and fair disclosure of assets and liabilities by both parties, and be entered into voluntarily, preferably with each party having the benefit of independent legal counsel.29 Crucially, like any agreement between nonmarital partners, it cannot be based on meretricious consideration.27 The agreement can delineate ownership of property brought into the relationship, specify how jointly acquired assets will be treated, and outline responsibilities for shared expenses.29

The widespread acceptance and enforceability of cohabitation agreements provide a powerful analogy for the present analysis. These agreements prove that the legal system is capable of severing the economic aspects of a relationship from the personal and sexual ones for the purpose of legal enforcement. If the law can uphold a comprehensive agreement governing all financial aspects of a couple's life together, it logically follows that it possesses the analytical tools to evaluate other, more limited contracts for services between individuals who might also engage in sexual activity. The existence of this legal framework normalizes the concept of analyzing economic exchanges independently of the sexual dynamics of a relationship, reinforcing the idea that not all financial arrangements between sexually intimate individuals are reducible to a prohibited contract for sex.

title.emphasis: 2.3 The Illegality of "Naive" Prostitution Contracts: Void as Against Public Policy

section.para: While the Marvin doctrine opens the door to a wide range of enforceable agreements, it also implicitly clarifies why what might be termed a "naive prostitution contract" is a legal nullity. Any express contract where the consideration is explicitly defined as the performance of a "lewd act" for money, as proscribed by Penal Code § 647(b), is void on its face. Such an agreement is a contract to commit a criminal act, rendering it unenforceable as a matter of fundamental public policy.34 The attempt to cloak an illegal transaction in the language of contract law is legally futile.

The very act of trying to formalize an agreement for prostitution highlights the fundamental distinction between a legally cognizable contract and a criminal conspiracy. A contract requires a lawful object; an agreement to commit a crime has an unlawful object by definition. Therefore, the "cover up" of attempting to legitimize an illegal act through contractual formalities not only fails to make the agreement enforceable but can also create additional legal liabilities, such as fraud or conspiracy. This legal absurdity underscores the point that prostitution, as defined by the penal code, occupies a narrow and specific category of prohibited conduct. It is not part of the broader universe of "contracts for sexual services" that might be subject to analysis under principles of contract law. Instead, it is an illegal act, and an agreement to perform it is simply evidence of a conspiracy to commit a crime, not a contract to be interpreted or enforced. This reinforces the clean separation between the narrow criminal act of prostitution and the much broader, and legally complex, category of compensated sexual services.

title.emphasis: 2.4 A Proposed Taxonomy of Compensated Sexual Service Contracts

section.para: By synthesizing the narrow definition of criminal prostitution from the Penal Code with the principles of severable consideration from Marvin v. Marvin, it is possible to construct a taxonomy of compensated sexual service contracts, categorizing them based on their potential legality and enforceability. This framework moves beyond a simple legal/illegal binary and provides a more nuanced understanding of the legal landscape.

listitem.para: Category 1: Illicit and Unenforceable (Criminal Prostitution). This category includes any agreement, express or implied, where the bargained-for consideration is the performance of a "lewd act" (the touching of genitals, buttocks, or female breast for sexual arousal) in exchange for money or other compensation. This is the precise conduct criminalized by PC § 647(b). Such agreements are not merely unenforceable; they constitute the crime of soliciting or agreeing to engage in prostitution and are void ab initio.

Category 2: Licit but Unenforceable (Gratuitous Exchange). This category covers arrangements within an intimate relationship where services, including sexual intimacy, are exchanged without the intent to create a legally binding contract. Courts often presume that services rendered between cohabitating partners are done out of "love and affection" and are therefore gratuitous gifts, not part of a bargained-for exchange that would give rise to contractual rights.35 While the relationship is licit, there is no enforceable contract for the services.

Category 3: Licit and Enforceable (Severable Consideration). This is the classic Marvin agreement. It involves an express or implied contract between nonmarital partners where the consideration is for lawful services that are severable from the sexual aspect of the relationship. Examples include compensation or property sharing in exchange for one partner's contributions as a homemaker, business partner, financial manager, or companion in a non-sexual sense.23 The sexual relationship exists alongside the contract but is not the consideration for it.

Category 4: Potentially Licit and Enforceable (Non-Proscribed Sexual Services). This theoretical category represents the frontier of the analysis. It encompasses contracts for compensated sexual services that do not fall within the narrow definition of criminal prostitution. The legality and enforceability of these agreements would depend on a detailed analysis of public policy and their specific nature, particularly through the lens of First Amendment protections for expressive conduct. This category could include contracts for services such as:
Performance Art: Hiring an actor to perform in a non-obscene but sexually explicit work, as protected by the Freeman decision.
Sexual Surrogacy: Engaging a surrogate partner for therapeutic purposes under the guidance of a licensed therapist, where the goal is treatment, not prurient gratification.
Educational Modeling: Paying a model to participate in the creation of materials for sex education or medical training.

section.para: The existence of this fourth category, however theoretical, demonstrates that the statement "contracting for sexual services is illegal" is imprecise. The law, in fact, requires a highly specific inquiry into the nature of the act, the intent of the parties, and the context of the transaction to determine legality.
